questions.question: What is the purpose of the __all__ list in a module?
choices: It lists all classes in the module
It defines which names are exported when a client uses 'from module import *'
It prevents the module from being imported
questions.explanation: __all__ is a list of strings that explicitly declares the public API of a module. When a client runs 'from module import *', only the names listed in __all__ are copied. Without it, all names not starting with an underscore are exported.

questions.question: The idiom 'if __name__ == "__main__":' is used to run code only when the file is executed as a ___, not when it is imported.
questions.explanation: When Python runs a file directly, it sets __name__ to '__main__'. When the same file is imported as a module, __name__ is set to the module's name. This guard lets you include test code or a CLI entry point that only runs on direct execution.

questions.question: What is the convention for single-underscore prefixed names like _helper in a module?
choices: They signal the name is internal and will be skipped by 'from module import *' if __all__ is not defined
They are automatically deleted after import
They are only accessible from within the same class
questions.explanation: A single leading underscore (e.g., _helper) is a naming convention that signals a name is intended for internal use. If __all__ is not defined, 'from module import *' will skip these names, though they can still be imported explicitly.

questions.question: What does 'from __future__ import annotations' do?
choices: Makes all annotations lazily evaluated as strings instead of being resolved at definition time
questions.explanation: Future imports activate features planned for later Python versions. 'from __future__ import annotations' (PEP 563) makes all type annotations strings by default, enabling forward references and reducing import-time overhead.

questions.question: A module attribute named __all__ must be a list of ___.
questions.explanation: __all__ must be a list of strings, where each string is the name of an attribute to export. For example: __all__ = ['parse', 'render', 'Config']. Non-string entries will cause an error during 'from module import *'.

questions.question: Which is a recommended module design pattern for large projects?
choices: Put all code in a single large module for simplicity
Minimize cross-module coupling: modules should import what they need and expose a clear public API via __all__ or naming conventions
Use 'from module import *' everywhere to keep import lines short
Avoid splitting code into modules until the project exceeds 10,000 lines
questions.explanation: Good module design minimizes coupling between modules. Each module should have a clear purpose, import only what it needs, and expose a well-defined public API. This makes code easier to understand, test, and maintain.
